name: The Trees of Elm Row
description: A tiny field guide to the trees on one street — the sample grammar that ships with this starter. Replace it with a library about YOUR thing: a lineage, a language, a valley's folk songs, a family's recipes. The shape stays the same; only the subject changes.

sections.What it is: A broad white oak (*Quercus alba*), maybe eighty years old, standing where the pavement bulges around its roots.
sections.Where: In front of number 12, the tallest thing on the block.
sections.In each season: Bare and architectural in winter; a green vault by June; in autumn it drops acorns the squirrels bury in every planter on the row.
sections.Note: The kind of item you'd write about your own subject — a plain observation, in your own words. No fortune, no claim. Just the record.

items.name: The Red Oak on the Corner

sections.What it is: A northern red oak (*Quercus rubra*), younger and straighter than its white cousin, with leaves that turn a hard scarlet.
sections.Where: The corner of Elm Row and Vine, shading the bus stop.
sections.In each season: Last to leaf out in spring, last to let go in autumn — it holds its brown leaves half the winter.

sections.What it is: A single ginkgo (*Ginkgo biloba*), a living fossil with fan-shaped leaves — a species older than the dinosaurs, planted here by the city.
sections.Where: Halfway down, outside the old bakery.
sections.In each season: For one week each November it turns pure gold and drops every leaf at once, overnight, into a perfect ring.

sections.About this group: A composite item — it holds no tree of its own; it *emerges* from the two oaks by listing their ids in `composite_of`. This is how a library grows structure without a database: an item that groups other items. In the tree view it becomes a branch; in the card view it's a card you can open to see its members.
sections.Why it matters: Emergence is the whole trick. You write leaves (the individual things); groupings like this one let a pattern across them become its own object you can point at, link to, and talk about.
